The performance of each of those different models so you can make that decision in an informed way.
And so we're going to use two tools to help us with those knobs and understanding how those two things work, cross validation and regularization.

If I have a model that's going to predict how home values in Chicago for the CCAO.

I want to use that model next year when it comes time to audit the houses and assess them and figure out how much each one is worth. Right? Yeah, sure. It's nice to know how well I do in the past. But really what I want is I want for that model to be useful in the future. And in the future, I'm going to get data that I have never seen before. And that's the whole point of this process is to mimic how do we simulate the idea of seeing data and testing our model on data that we have never seen before.
Because that's how the models are going to be used in the real world.

Every regression model that I have ever trained and used has been then deployed on data that it never saw during training, right? And we want to make sure that when whoever's responsible for using the decisions that come out of that model, they want to know that they can trust it. They want to know that it's going to do the right thing. And so we have to have some way of convincing them based on the data that we have.
So if we make this super complex model, it's not going to generalize well to new data, right? It's too complex and we said this is overfit to the training data.

Like, OK, figure out the coefficients, the thetas so that we can make our predictions. And then our validation set is what we're going to use to help us tune the knobs of our model. Do we want like a quadratic? Do we want a cubic? What kind of do we want these features? Those features, how do we decide?

We're going to use the validation set to help us decide. And then at the very, very end, once and one time only, we're going to run our model on the test set and use that as a measure of how well we think it's going to do going forward in the future on new data that it's never seen before.
And so because we want to simulate that process of new data that we've never seen before, we have to be very careful to lock our test set down and never touch it during that process.

So this is super important for model evaluation. You know, in the process of fitting your model, don't use your test set, don't use it to choose number of parameters, don't even use it to figure out the means, the averages to, you know, standardize your data. The data set changes a little.

So I'm going to briefly talk about some pitfalls before we move on. So couple things. One thing, what if our data set happens to be sorted right? We have a bunch of points that are in sorted order. Well, the whole point of cross validation was to say, well, we don't want to like get stuck with one particularly bad or unrepresentative validation set. Just to put value from this to this right if it's sorted on one variable?

For example, let's suppose we have data in pairs, right? We're trying to predict total household household income of individuals and we do a survey. But it turns out in our survey, we surveyed a bunch of people who are married to each other.

So that means if I train a model.

On the people, some of the people in some of the households and then try to evaluate it on some of the people in the same households, right? Like I train a model and one of the data points is me, and then I try to evaluate it on my partner.

Well, that's not fair because we have the same household income, we have the same pattern. And so it's not a fair evaluation because in the future, that model is not going to be used on the spouses, the partners and the people in my data set. It's going to be used on an entirely new people. So that's an unfair evaluation. It's going to make our validation or test error look unreasonably high.
Right, because it's going to be like, Oh yeah, like your errors or unreasonably low, right? It's going to be like, Oh yeah, your model is great. Really low error. Looks fantastic.

If there are blocks, if there are groups, we always sort of keep everybody in the group in either the training or the validation set.
What if both of the above are true? For example, what happens if you have a time series?
Well it doesn't really make sense to like take a time you know the data .1 for every day, train on some of the days and then predict the others because you know day-to-day things are similar.

So how might we get around this? Well thankfully scikit learn has something called time series cross validation and the way people do this is the first fold. We train on the 1st chunk of the data and validate on the next chunk.

The second time we train on this set of the data and validate on this chunk and so on and so on and so on.
